title: Queen Abla Pokou: The Mother of the Baoule People
summary: In the 18th century, a great crisis divided the Ashanti Kingdom in what is now Ghana.
full_text:
In the 18th century, a great crisis divided the Ashanti Kingdom in what is now Ghana. A civil war forced a group of Ashanti to flee their homeland. They were led by a woman of extraordinary courage: Princess Abla Pokou, niece of the Ashanti king.

Abla Pokou led her people westward, through the dense forests of West Africa, searching for a new homeland. They traveled for months, pursued by enemies, surviving on roots and wild game. Hundreds died along the way. But Abla Pokou never wavered.

They reached the Komoe River. It was the rainy season, and the river was swollen to a raging torrent a mile wide. There were no boats, no bridges. The pursuers were closing in. The people despaired.

Abla Pokou consulted the priest. The priest communed with the spirits and delivered a terrible message: "The river will not let you pass unless you sacrifice the most precious thing you possess. You must throw your most valuable child into the water."

The people looked at each other. Which child would be chosen? Who would volunteer their son or daughter?

Abla Pokou stepped forward. She held her infant son in her arms, her only child. She looked at the raging river, then at her people. Without a word, she threw her son into the water.

The river parted. The people crossed on dry ground. On the other side, Abla Pokou turned to look back at the river where her son had drowned. She cried out: "Ba-ou-li!" — "the child is dead!"

From that cry came the name of a new people: the Baoule. Abla Pokou became the first queen of the Baoule nation. Her sacrifice became the founding story of a people.

Today, Abla Pokou is remembered as the mother of the Baoule, one of the largest ethnic groups in Ivory Coast. Her statue stands in the capital Yamoussoukro. Her story is taught in every Ivorian school. The sacrifice of her son is not seen as a tragedy but as the ultimate act of leadership — the willingness to give everything for the survival of the people.

The story of Abla Pokou raises questions that every leader must face: what are you willing to sacrifice? How much do you love your people? The answer, for Abla Pokou, was everything.
moral: True leadership requires the willingness to sacrifice everything for the people.
